exemplar.title: Investigating the relationship between induced voltage and the rate of change of magnetic field through a solenoid.

detailedExemplarData.title: Investigating the relationship between induced voltage and the rate of change of magnetic field through a solenoid.

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data tables and graphs include correct headings, units and uncertainty notation with clear figure captions
Appropriate uncertainty calculations (instrumental and spread-based) and uncertainty bounds on the best-fit line
Linear regression well executed, demonstrating the expected v â âh relationship
weaknesses: Typographical slips and inconsistent significant figures reduce precision slightly
Height measurement uncertainty was omitted and one percentage uncertainty was mis-copied
Some uncertainty propagation methods (range/2, averaging) are unconventional
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication of data recording and processing is clear and largely precise, with only minor formatting and labeling inconsistencies. Although uncertainties are considered, there are notable omissions and mis-calculations that prevent a flawless treatment. This performance sits solidly in the 5-6 band but falls short of full mastery, justifying a score of 5.

strengths: Research question embedded in a highly specific and appropriate context
Procedure fully reproducible with annotated photographs, variable tables and clear rationale
Comprehensive consideration of controls and multiple-trial approach reducing random error
weaknesses: Minor omissions in coil dimensions and data-logger settings prevent absolutely flawless reproducibility
Quantitative justification for neglecting air resistance is missing
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands of the Research Design criterion demonstrate performance at the very top of the 5-6 band. The research question is situated in an explicit, focused context and the methodology is described in sufficient detail to allow accurate reproduction. Methodological considerations are thoroughly explained, linking procedural choices to error reduction. Given that only very minor details (e.g., exact coil dimensions, data-logger settings) are absent, the work merits the maximum score of 6.

strengths: Conclusion is fully consistent with the analysis and correctly references the best-fit gradient and RÂ²
Clear discussion linking results to Faradayâs law and energy conservation
Logical restatement of the v â âh relationship
weaknesses: No quantitative comparison of the experimental gradient to a theoretical or literature value
Could deepen scientific context by benchmarking against expected theoretical predictions
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is justified and consistent with the processed data, and the student makes a relevant comparison to accepted scientific context. However, the absence of any numerical or theoretical benchmarking prevents elevation to the 6-point band. The work is therefore awarded 5 points.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses (angle variation, reading errors, air resistance) are identified
Realistic and relevant improvements (mechanical release, double-checking readings) are proposed
Insightful suggestions for extension and future work
weaknesses: The relative impact of each weakness on the final gradient is not quantified
Some improvement suggestions lack explanation of how they would tighten uncertainties or alter results
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation goes beyond generic statements by describing specific limitations and proposing realistic, relevant improvements. While the student stops short of explaining the quantitative impact of each weakness, the depth and applicability of their suggestions position the work in the top half of the 3-4 band, warranting a score of 5.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question (effect of drop height on peak induced voltage in a 600-turn solenoid) is stated early and embedded within a clear discussion of electromagnetic induction, Faradayâs law and practical motivation.  The student narrows the context to a specific coil, height range and measurable voltage, demonstrating an appropriate and focused setting.  Minor generic phrasing in the opening paragraph prevents a flawless 6, but the context is nevertheless explicit and specific.

Instrumental (Â±0.01 V, Â±0.01 m) and spread-based uncertainties are calculated, percentage uncertainties tabulated, and gradient range used.  However, some values are mis-copied (e.g., 0.2375 vs 0.2475) and height uncertainty is ignored.  The approach is appropriate but contains notable omissions, fitting the 3-4 band; quality is nearer the top of that band.

Key considerationsâalignment tube, constant coil, background magnetic fields, multiple trials, instrumental resolutionâare explained with rationale linking them to flux change or measurement error.  The quantity and range of data (10 heights Ã10 trials) are sufficient to address the question.  Explanations are present but occasionally brief, hence 5.

Specific weaknesses (angle variation, reading errors, air resistance) are listed and briefly linked to possible voltage changes, but the relative magnitude or detailed effect on the final gradient is not explained.  This is âdescribedâ rather than âexplainedâ, placing it in the 3-4 band at the lower score of 3.

A step-by-step procedure, annotated photograph, full material list and variable tables allow another student to repeat the investigation with very few questions.  Heights, number of trials, coil turns, voltmeter range and orientation are included.  Some details (coil dimensions, exact data-logger settings) are absent, so reproduction is highly probable but not absolutely guaranteeed, justifying 5 rather than 6.

Means, induced-voltage range, linear regression and gradient uncertainty are executed correctly in concept, providing a valid v â âh relationship.  Nonetheless, the induced-voltage ârange/2â definition is unconventional, and one uncertainty percentage is mis-calculated.  These inaccuracies place the work in the 3-4 band, at 4.

Improvements such as a release mechanism and double-checking readings are realistic and relevant, yet the student does not elaborate on how much they would tighten uncertainties or alter the outcome.  The suggestions are described, not fully explained, matching 3 points in the 3-4 band.

Raw tables carry correct headings, units and uncertainty notation; processed means and error bars appear on the graph; figure captions are present.  Minor typographical slips (mixed capitalisation, units in headings) limit precision slightly, so 5 is more defensible than 6.

The student links results to Faradayâs law and Lenzâs law, explaining polarity and proportionality, which is a relevant comparison.  However, no quantitative theoretical gradient or literature value is presented for deeper benchmarking, so the comparison is partial rather than fully justified, aligning with the 3-4 range.

The conclusion restates the obtained linear relation, references the best-fit gradient and RÂ², and ties the finding directly to Faradayâs law and energy conservation.  It is fully consistent with processed data.  Minor lack of numerical comparison of gradient to a theoretical value keeps it at 5 rather than 6.

annotations.comment: The research question is well situated within a focused context, specifying the range of heights and solenoid parameters, enabling a reader to understand the investigationâs scope and practical relevance.
The introduction includes personal motivation but spends substantial space on relevance, slightly diluting the scientific context; consider tightening to emphasize theoretical background.
The statement of Faradayâs law is clear, but linking this equation directly to the expected ââh relationship here would strengthen the logical flow into the hypothesis.
The derivation of the flux expression is comprehensive and illustrates all terms; this clarity supports reproducibility of the theoretical framework.
When neglecting air resistance in the energy calculation, the student should justify its insignificance quantitatively, especially at lower drop heights where drag may be non-negligible.
The hypothesis is well articulated, linking theoretical vââh to practical induction; the explicit prediction demonstrates strong alignment with Faradayâs law.
The variable table clearly identifies independent and dependent variables but should include measurement uncertainties and instrument resolution to enhance reproducibility.
The control-variables table lists maintenance of coil position but omits coil dimensions; specify coil geometry (length, diameter) as it directly affects magnetic flux linkage.
The description of the paper tube alignment is apt but lists length as approximate; record exact internal and external dimensions to ensure experimental repeatability.
Materials list is comprehensive, but the digital voltmeterâs sampling rate and data-logger settings are not specified; include these to allow precise replication.
The pre-data collection check using the south pole promotes consistency in polarity; this procedural control is a strong methodological consideration.
Recording ten trials per height effectively reduces random error and increases reliability; this multiple-trial approach enhances data quality.
The manual release of the magnet introduces variability; consider a mechanical release mechanism to standardize initial conditions and reduce angle inconsistency.
The graph of v vs. time is informative but lacks explicit axis labels and units; include clear axis headings and gridlines for full precision.
The discussion of polarity inversion when dropping opposite poles is clear; however, quantify any small asymmetries observed to support your claims.
The raw data table is well structured but shows inconsistent decimal places; maintain uniform significant figures across all entries for clarity.
The captions for raw Vmin data are minimal; add a numbered table caption and ensure it matches the label in the text for coherence.
Referencing colored markers on the graph is useful, but describe the color scheme consistently in the figure legend to avoid ambiguity.
One percentage uncertainty (30.22% at 0.05 m) appears inconsistent with the range calculation; recheck rounding and ensure that copied values match processed data.
The use of range/2 for uncertainty is acceptable, but supplementing this with standard deviation would provide a more robust estimate of spread.
Height measurement uncertainty is disregarded in voltage analysis; include ruler precision in propagation of uncertainties to fully account for error sources.
In the induced voltage range formula, ensure absolute values are applied consistently so that negative minima do not lead to sign errors.
Averaging the uncertainties of Vmax and Vmin by simple mean neglects their individual weighting; consider full uncertainty propagation for the combined range.
The percentage uncertainty calculation uses 0.2375 but the earlier uncertainty was 0.2475, resulting in a misestimate; verify consistency of referenced values.
The best-fit plot is annotated effectively with uncertainty bounds; axis labeling of âh (m^1/2) could be improved to indicate units clearly.
Discussing the y-intercept origin error is insightful; extend this by estimating its magnitude relative to coil sensitivity or background flux to quantify systematic bias.
Reference to RÂ² is appropriate; consider adding the p-value or confidence interval for the gradient to bolster statistical interpretation.
Gradient uncertainty is correctly found as Â±0.63 V mâ»Â¹/Â²; enhancing this by a formal propagation analysis would provide a more rigorous error estimate.
The evaluation insightfully identifies high uncertainty at small heights; strengthen by quantifying how this uncertainty propagates to the final gradient.
The conclusion effectively restates the vââh relationship but omits comparison of the experimental gradient (1.93 V mâ»Â¹/Â²) to a theoretical estimate; include this for full justification.
The weaknesses table articulates potential errors but lacks quantification of their relative impact on gradient or RÂ²; add estimated percentage impacts to explain significance.
The single strength entry highlights use of the south pole but omits other procedural strengths such as multiple trials and systematic data logging; consider expanding this section.
Extension ideas of varying coil voltage and magnet type are well chosen; strengthen by outlining expected trends or numerical predictions to guide future work.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â

similarExemplars.title: I will investigate the relationship between the enclosed current in a solenoid (27.1mA, 40.2mA, 53.7mA, 80.4mA, 106.7mA), and the magnetic field strength produced by that solenoid (mT). The solenoid will be consistent each trial, consisting of an insulated copper wire wrapped 60 times around an iron core that has a radius of 1cm and length of 15cm.

userSubmission.researchQuestion: I will investigate the relationship between the enclosed current in a solenoid (27.1mA, 40.2mA, 53.7mA, 80.4mA, 106.7mA), and the magnetic field strength produced by that solenoid (mT). The solenoid will be consistent each trial, consisting of an insulated copper wire wrapped 60 times around an iron core that has a radius of 1cm and length of 15cm.
